name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٦٠)
```

```english
The people of Lot impugned the apostles (160)
```

```arabic
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ لُوطٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٦١)
```

```english
when Lot, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (161)
```

```arabic
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٦٢)
```

```english
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (162)
```

```arabic
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٦٣)
```

```english
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (163)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦٤)
```

```english
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (164)
```

```arabic
أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلذُّكْرَانَ مِنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦٥)
```

```english
What! Of all people do you come to males, (165)
```

```arabic
وَتَذَرُونَ مَا خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ عَادُونَ (١٦٦)
```

```english
abandoning your wives your Lord has created for you? Indeed, you are a transgressing lot.’ (166)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَـٰلُوطُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُخْرَجِينَ (١٦٧)
```

```english
They said, ‘Lot, if you do not desist, you will surely be banished.’ (167)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنِّى لِعَمَلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْقَالِينَ (١٦٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘Indeed I detest your conduct.’ (168)
```

```arabic
رَبِّ نَجِّنِى وَأَهْلِى مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٦٩)
```

```english
‘My Lord! Deliver me and my family from what they do.’ (169)
```

```arabic
فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٧٠)
```

```english
So We delivered him and all his family, (170)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا عَجُوزًا فِى ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (١٧١)
```

```english
except an old woman who remained behind. (171)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (١٧٢)
```

```english
Then We destroyed [all] the rest, (172)
```

```arabic
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَسَآءَ مَطَرُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (١٧٣)
```

```english
and rained down upon them a rain [of stones]. Evil was the rain of those who were warned! (173)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٧٤)
```

```english
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (174)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٧٥)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (175)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses refer to the story of the Prophet Lot (peace be upon him) (لوط عليه‌السلام), who came after Salih (peace be upon him) (صالح عليه‌السلام).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** — up to His saying — **رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** "The people of Lot impugned the apostles... Lord of all the worlds." The explanation of this has preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَتَأْتُونَ الذُّكْرانَ مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ** "What! Do you come to males from among all the worlds?" (26:165). The interrogative is for denunciation (الإنكار) and rebuke (التوبيخ). The term "males" (الذكران) is the plural of "male" (ذكر), the opposite of female. "Coming to them" (إتيانهم) is a metaphor for sodomy (اللواط), which had become widespread among them. "All the worlds" (العالمين) is the plural of "world" (عالم), referring here to groups of people.

His saying: **مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ** "from among all the worlds" could be connected to the pronoun of the subject in **تَأْتُونَ** "you come," meaning: "Do you, from among all the worlds, commit this heinous act?" In this case, it would carry the meaning of His saying in another place: **ما سَبَقَكُمْ بِها مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ** "No one in the world has ever preceded you in this" (7:80, 29:28).

Alternatively, it could be connected to His saying: **الذُّكْرانَ** "the males." The meaning would then be: "Do you, from among all the worlds—despite their vast numbers and the inclusion of women—marry only the men?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَتَذَرُونَ ما خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ** "and leave what your Lord has created for you of your wives?..." (26:166). The word **تَذَرُونَ** means "you leave" or "you abandon," and it has no past tense form derived from its own root.

One who contemplates the creation of man and the division of its individuals into the two categories of male and female—and the organs and instruments with which each category is equipped, as well as the specificities of their creation—will not doubt that the purpose of the Fashioner and Originator (الصنع والإيجاد) in this diverse formation, and in placing the instinct of desire (غريزة الشهوة) in both groups and differentiating their roles through action (الفعل) and reception (الانفعال), was to bring them together through marriage (النكاح). This serves as a means for procreation (التناسل), which preserves the survival of the species (بقاء النوع) until a destined time.

Thus, the human male, insofar as he is a male, is created for the female, not for a male like himself; and the human female, insofar as she is a female, is created for the male, not for a female like herself. What is specific to the male in his creation is for the female, and what is specific to the female in her creation is for the male. This is the natural pairing (الزوجية الطبيعية) which the Fashioner and Originator (الصنع والإيجاد) contracted between the human male and female, making them a pair (زوجين).

Subsequently, social or religious aims and ends established among people the custom of social, conventional marriage (النكاح الاجتماعي الاعتباري), which involves a type of exclusivity between the spouses and a form of regulation for the aforementioned natural pairing. Thus, the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and the specific creation (الخلقة) guide man toward the pairing of men with women, not men with men, and the pairing of women with men, not women with women. Furthermore, marriage is built upon the principle of procreation and reproduction (التوالد والتناسل), rather than mere partnership in life in a general sense.

From this, it becomes clear that the most likely meaning of His saying: **ما خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ** "what your Lord has created for you" refers to the organ permitted for men from women through marriage. The preposition "for" (اللام) denotes natural possession (الملك الطبيعي). The word **مِنْ** "of" in His saying: **مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ** "of your wives" is for partitivity (التبعيض), and the "pairing" (الزوجية) here refers to natural pairing, although it is possible to interpret it as social, conventional pairing in a certain sense.

As for the suggestion by some that the relative pronoun **ما** "what" refers to the women themselves, and that His saying **مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ** "of your wives" is an explanation (بيان) for it, this is far-fetched.

His saying: **بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ عادُونَ** "Rather you are a transgressing people" (26:166). That is, you are those who overstep and depart from the boundary delineated for you by the primordial nature (الفطرة) and creation (الخلقة). It carries the same meaning as His saying: **إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجالَ وَتَقْطَعُونَ السَّبِيلَ** "Do you indeed come to men and cut off the way?" (29:29).

It has become evident from all that has passed that the speech of Lot (peace be upon him) is built upon a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) that has been alluded to.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ يا لُوطُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُخْرَجِينَ** "They said, ‘If you do not desist, O Lot, you will surely be among the expelled’" (26:167). That is, those who are distanced and banished from our town, as is narrated from them in another place: **أَخْرِجُوا آلَ لُوطٍ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ** "Expel the family of Lot from your town" (27:56).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ إِنِّي لِعَمَلِكُمْ مِنَ الْقالِينَ** "He said, ‘I am indeed one of those who detest your conduct’" (26:168). What is meant by "their conduct" (عملهم)—as indicated by the context—is their coming to males and abandoning females. The term **القالي** means the one who detests or hates. Meeting their threat of banishment with such a statement, without addressing the threat itself, conveys the meaning: "I do not fear being expelled from your town, nor do I care for it; rather, I detest your conduct and desire salvation from its evil consequence (وبال) which will inevitably descend upon you." For this reason, he followed it with his saying: **رَبِّ نَجِّنِي وَأَهْلِي مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ** "My Lord! Deliver me and my family from what they do" (26:169).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **رَبِّ نَجِّنِي وَأَهْلِي مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ** "My Lord! Deliver me and my family from what they do." This means either from the essence of the act they perform within his sight and hearing—as he was repulsed by it—or from the evil consequence of their act and the punishment (العذاب) that would inevitably follow it.

He mentioned only himself and his family because not a single person from the inhabitants of the town had believed in him. The Exalted said regarding this: **فَما وَجَدْنا فِيها غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ** "but We did not find in it more than one house of Muslims" (51:36).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَنَجَّيْناهُ وَأَهْلَهُ أَجْمَعِينَ** — up to His saying — **الْآخَرِينَ** "So We delivered him and his family, all of them... the others" (26:170-172). The term "the one who remained behind" (الغابر) means, as has been said, the one who stays after those who were with them have departed. "Destruction" (التدمير) means annihilation (الإهلاك). The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مَطَراً** "And We rained down upon them a rain..." (26:173). This refers to the baked clay (السجيل), as the Exalted said: **وَأَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ حِجارَةً مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ** "and We rained down upon them stones of baked clay" (15:74).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً** — up to His saying — **الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** "There is indeed a sign in that... the All-mighty, the All-merciful" (26:174-175). The explanation of this has preceded.
